data.title: Western NY City Most Competitive in U.S. Home Market — Market Talk
data.description:
1104 ET - Irondequoit, N.Y., a suburb of Rochester, is the most competitive housing market in the U.S., according to Redfin. Homes in the lakeside town typically sell in just 8.5 days and for well over their asking price. Four of the top 10 most competitive cities were in California, and three were in western New York. Every city has less than a month of supply, and in most cases, over half of homes sell within two weeks. Nationwide, the typical house sells in a month and a half. For the past few years, housing has been a waiting game, as buyers balk at record-high prices and sellers hold off on listing their homes until activity returns. In November, prices rose to a new monthly high, the typical home took 7 days longer to go under contract than a year prior, and new listings decreased to a new monthly low. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1058 ET - Price deflation for new homes was worse than expected for 2025 and further pressure on prices is expected in 2026, say analysts at Zelman & Associates. Zelman's proprietary measure of net new order prices was down 5% year-over-year in November--the largest decrease since July 2009. The price pressure has been driven by a combination of historically high levels of incentives as well as base price reductions. "Moving forward to 2026, a still-elevated inventory position during the key spring selling season is expected to result in continued price pressure," the analysts say, forecasting a 2% decline in home prices for the year. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1019 ET - The 0.3% decline in Canada's GDP in October came as no surprise to Royce Mendes, director of macro strategy at Desjardins Securities. He says strength in 3Q--of 2.6% at an annualized rate--"looked unsustainable" given that final domestic demand edged downward in the period. He says fixed-income traders--which made bets on rate hikes in the second half of 2026--put too much stock into that third-quarter GDP report and also job gains reported in Statistics Canada's Labour Force Survey, which Mendes says has a history of volatility. Mendes expects weakness in economic activity to persist through early 2026. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1018 ET - Oil futures edge down after making big gains yesterday on the U.S.'s tightening of the blockade of tankers entering or leaving Venezuela. Ritterbusch and Associates sees Russia-Ukraine issues, with the possibility of further U.S. sanctions and strikes on Russian infrastructure as a bigger consideration for oil prices. "The U.S. quarantine on oil tanker traffic in and out of Venezuela has occupied media headlines of late, but the quantity of crude disruption to the global market remains small in our opinion," the firm says. WTI is off 0.3% at $57.84 a barrel, and Brent is down 0.4% at $61.83.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0954 ET - Data indicating a 0.3% drop in Canadian GDP in October suggest that traders got ahead of themselves by betting on Bank of Canada interest-rate increases in the second half of 2026, says Stephen Brown, economist at Capital Economics. The report on October was filled with bad news, given broad-based weakness, he says. Exacerbating matters is the early estimate for a 0.1% jump in November, which Brown argues was "the least we had expected" given the end of a teachers' strike in Alberta. He estimates GDP in December needs to grow 0.3% month-over-month to avoid a negative fourth-quarter print. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0950 ET - There were an estimated 37.2% more home sellers than buyers in the U.S. housing market in November, Redfin says. That's 529,770 more in numerical terms. When sellers outnumber buyers, buyers typically hold the negotiating power because they have a lot of options to choose from. But that's only in a buyer's market for those who can afford to buy. Currently, many Americans have been priced out of the housing market as affordability has eroded. The number of home buyers in the U.S. housing market dropped 2.5% month over month in November. The number of sellers in the market fell 1.4% month over month to 1.95 million. Buyers are backing off due to high housing costs and economic uncertainty. Sellers, many of whom are buyers themselves, are backing off in response to lackluster demand for their homes. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0948 ET - The Canadian economy has some work to do to avoid a negative GDP reading in the fourth quarter after a weak October, says Robert Kavcic, economist at BMO Capital Markets. GDP in October declined 0.3% from the previous month, and Statistics Canada's early estimate for November indicates a meager 0.1% jump in November. October "wasn't a month to write home about for the Canadian economy," Kavcic says. The result likely dampens recent enthusiasm about the Canadian economy shaking off the worse of the impact from hefty U.S. tariffs on key sector manufacturing sectors. In the third quarter, Canada recorded a surprise 2.6% annualized increase, albeit skewed by a decline in imports. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0946 ET — Despite questions about the macro environment and overall housing demand, home sales should still increase at a low single-digit rate in 2026 and 2027, say analysts at Zelman & Associates. The analysts' forecast assumes improved affordability from reduced home prices and gradually declining interest rates. But even with modest volume growth, builders could continue to face pressure on price and margin, with many positioned for inventory accumulation in the coming year. Meanwhile, lower interest rates or new government programs to improve affordability and buyer demand could lead to high single-digit to low double-digit volume growth, but if the economy enters a recession, sales could fall by mid-to-high single digits. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0936 ET - The Canadian economy struggled early in the fourth quarter, with broad-based weakness in October pointing to a modest GDP contraction for the October-to-December period, says Andrew Grantham, economist at CIBC Capital Markets. GDP on an industry basis fell 0.3% in October from the previous month. There were one-off factors like labor disputes among teachers in western Canada and postal workers. And maintenance work in the oil patch contributed to a decline in the commodities sector, Grantham says. Manufacturing, wholesaling and retailing recorded declines, and Statistics Canada's early estimate for November--a 0.1% gain--suggests only a partial recovery. However, weak momentum would have to drag into early 2026 to reignite talk of Bank of Canada rate cuts, Grantham says. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0925 ET - Canada's economy contracted in October, and Statistics Canada points to two labor disruptions that weighed on results. The educational-services component fell 1.8% in the month due to a weekslong teachers' strike in the western Canadian province of Alberta. Overall, the public-sector aggregate--which comprises education, healthcare, and public administration--declined 0.3%. Meanwhile, a component covering logistics dropped 1.1%, and Statistics Canada attributes the decrease to disruptions in mail and parcel deliveries stemming from strikes at Canada Post. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0549 ET - Novo Nordisk should benefit from U.S. approval of a pill version of Wegovy, AJ Bell's Russ Mould writes in a note. U.S. regulators approved the first GLP-1 weight-loss pill, a tablet formulation of Novo Nordisk's Ozempic and Wegovy. The decision gives the Danish drugmaker an early advantage in the race to bring an oral obesity treatment to market, he says. "This is a significant breakthrough given the reluctance of some people to use the existing injectable option and it could help the company win back market share from [Eli] Lilly," he adds. While rival Eli Lilly is expected to secure approval for its own pill in the near future, the development is a timely boost for new CEO Mike Doustdar as he works to revive Novo's fortunes. Shares are up 7.6% at 326.50 Danish kroner. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: GRANGEX Extends Loan Agreement with Orion until 31 May, 2026
data.description:
GRANGEX AB (publ) (”Grangex” or ”the Company”) is pleased to announce that the Company has executed an agreement with OMF Fund II H Ltd (”Orion”) to extend the maturity date of the existing US$22,500,000 loan facility (the “Orion Loan”) from 31 December 2025 to 31 May 2026.

As announced on 6 May 2024, the Orion Loan was assumed by the Company as part of the Company’s acquisition of Sydvaranger Mining AS, the 98.5% owner of the Sydvaranger Mine in Kirkenes (“Sydvaranger Mine”).

The Orion Loan repayment extension enables the Company to complete the project financing for restart of operations at the Sydvaranger Mine.  The project financing process is ongoing with assistance from three leading Nordic investment banks (DNB Carnegie, ABG Sundal Collier and SB1 Markets) and is now expected to close in the first quarter of 2026. The Company’s target to commence exports of ultra-high-grade direct reduction magnetite concentrate from the Sydvaranger Mine remains for the fourth quarter of 2026.

Christer Lindqvist, CEO of Grangex AB says: “We are very pleased to have executed this loan extension agreement with Orion. My thanks to the Orion team for their continued support as we work to complete the project financing to restart operations at the Sydvaranger Mine with a confirmed target to be in operation by end of 2026.”

Contacts

Christer Lindqvist, Chief Executive Officer, phone +46 70 591 04 83

About Us

GRANGEX is a Swedish mineral development company with the vision to actively contribute to a sustainable society within Europe. The company strives to become the foremost mineral development group in Europe, developing and conducting responsible extraction and recycling of minerals, primarily iron ore products and other critical minerals for the green transition. Through its products, GRANGEX will contribute to a reduced climate impact while minimising its own environmental and climate impact.

The company's share (short name GRANGX) is traded on Nasdaq First North Premier Growth Market Stockholm, with G&W Fondkommission as Certified Adviser.ISIN: SE0018014243For more information www.grangex.se/

This information is information that GRANGEX AB (publ.) is obliged to make public pursuant to the EU Market Abuse Regulation. The information was submitted for publication, through the agency of the contact persons set out above, at 2025-12-23 17:05 CET.

data.title: Yields rise, stocks up slightly after US GDP data; yen firms on currency warnings
data.description:
Key points:
  • S&P 500 up slightly in early trading
  • Yen gains, traders on alert to intervention risk
  • European shares hit record high

By Caroline Valetkevitch and Dhara Ranasinghe

Major stock indexes were up slightly and Treasury yields rose on Tuesday after stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data, while the yen shot up after warnings from Tokyo on its readiness to support the battered currency.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index briefly hit a record high on gains in the healthcare sector, after heavyweight Novo Nordisk secured U.S. approval of its weight-loss pill.

Silver hit a record high above $70 an ounce, while gold also touched a record.

Data showed the U.S. economy grew faster than expected in the third quarter, driven by robust consumer spending. Early estimates showed gross domestic product increased at a 4.3% annualized rate last quarter, far above economists' forecast for a rise at a 3.3% pace, according to a Reuters poll.

"We're still in this period of playing catch-up with economic data and GDP is dated... But it's showing strength in the economy, and strength that's above expectations," said Tim Ghriskey, senior portfolio strategist at Ingalls & Snyder in New York.

U.S. economic releases have been delayed by a record federal government shutdown.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 44.14 points, or 0.09%, to 48,406.82, the S&P 500 rose 5.95 points, or 0.09%, to 6,884.37 and the Nasdaq Composite rose 7.37 points, or 0.03%, to 23,435.22.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI rose 2.31 points, or 0.23%, to 1,017.80.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index was last up 0.4%. Japan's blue-chip Nikkei closed little changed on the day.

Novo Nordisk shares rallied on Tuesday after the U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved its weight-loss pill, giving the Danish drugmaker a competitive edge in the fast-evolving obesity treatment market.

The approval positions Novo Nordisk ahead in the race for a potent oral weight-loss medication as it works to recover market share lost to Eli Lilly .

The yield on 10-year Treasury notes was last up 2.71 basis points (bps) at 4.198%. The U.S. dollar curbed its losses against the yen and euro after the U.S. economic growth data, which reinforced expectations the Federal Reserve will pause cutting rates at its January meeting.

The yen was firm as investors weighed the odds of an imminent intervention from Japanese authorities to shore up the currency.

Japan has a free hand in dealing with excessive moves in the yen, Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Tuesday, issuing the strongest warning so far about Tokyo's readiness to intervene in the market to arrest sharp declines in the currency.

The Japanese yen strengthened 0.55% against the greenback to 156.21 per dollar. The euro was up 0.12% at $1.1775, while the dollar index was last down 0.19% at 98.05.

While the BOJ raised rates at its December policy meeting on Friday, the move was widely expected and Governor Kazuo Ueda offered few hints on the extent of future rate hikes.

The dollar slipped against other major currencies, with the euro up 0.3% at $1.1795 and sterling 0.4% higher at $1.3514.

Silver extended its record rally and hit the key $70 an ounce mark, while gold cut gains after setting an all-time high.

Spot silver rose 0.7% at $69.48 per ounce after touching a record high earlier. Spot gold was flat at $4,445.36.

U.S. crude fell 0.24% to $57.88 a barrel and Brent fell to $61.87 per barrel, down 0.32% on the day.

China's blue-chip CSI300 index rose 0.2%. China will step up urban renewal and deepen efforts to stabilize its property market in 2026 as it prepares to launch its latest Five-Year Plan, according to a readout of a housing policy work conference released on Tuesday.

data.title: Citing better cooperation, Montenegro lifts visas for Turkish citizens
data.description:
Montenegro on Tuesday revoked its decision to insist on visas for Turkish citizens and reintroduced a visa-free regime but for a shortened duration, citing improved cooperation with Turkish authorities.

Following violence triggered by the stabbing of a Montenegrin man in the capital Podgorica by a group of Turks, Montenegro in October suspended a regime that had allowed visa-free stays of up to 90 days for Turkish nationals.

"The material considered today states that cooperation with the Turkish authorities has been enhanced, alongside the implementation of strengthened security and migration checks," Montenegro's government said in a statement.

"The decision provides for a reduction of the permitted stay from 90 to 30 days, with a note that the visa regime may be reinstated in the event of an assessment of security risks."

The government said that by lifting visas for Turkish nationals it has aligned its policy with that of the European Union that it aims to join.

data.title: How to Make Your Economy-Class Seat a Little Less Miserable — WSJ
data.description:
By Mary Holland

Long-haul flights in economy class take a toll. To make your next flight less of a sufferfest, we tapped some frequent economy-class fliers for tips on making the back of the plane more tolerable — or even luxurious.

A winning strategy starts with seat selection. Content creator Monae Hendrickson, who has logged more than 130 hours in economy this year, always books the aisle, especially for night flights. "Having the freedom to get up and stretch whenever you want brings real peace of mind."

To optimize entertainment, Hendrickson pre-downloads movies on her devices, but also cues up an audiobook or two "for when my eyes need a break from screens." She still packs corded earphones for in-flight entertainment systems along with Apple AirPods Pro 3, which she loves for their size and noise-canceling capabilities.

Thankfully, more airlines are adding Bluetooth compatibility to screens, but meanwhile consider investing in the AirFly Pro 2, a tiny $60 dongle that plugs into the seat back screen and lets you use wireless headphones.

The undisputed best way to spend 13 hours in a cramped seat? Asleep. These days, travel-pillow options extend well beyond neck-brace-style cushions. Side-sleepers, for example, swear by the Trtl pillow, which wraps around your neck like a sturdy, supportive scarf. "I could never sleep on flights until I bought this pillow," said Hendrickson. "I love that it also holds my AirPods or my earplugs in my ears while [I'm] resting." Other options, like the Therm-a-Rest Lumbar Travel Pillow, are designed to be tucked behind your lower back.

Pack an easy-to-reach care kit, advises Thuy Hoang Anh Nguyen, who shares cost-saving travel inspiration on Instagram as @TravelsOnBudget. Along with snacks and electrolytes, Nguyen always brings a business-class-worthy toiletries bag that includes a toothbrush and face wipes. "Brushing my teeth midflight makes a real difference," she said.

One feature you'll see in business class seats that you'll seldom find in economy class: foot rests. Companies like Everlasting Comfort sell packable "foot hammocks," which can be hung from your tray table. If you're in an exit row, you might also have enough space to place a small inflatable footstool like the one from Sunany available on Amazon. Just make sure to check whether your airline allows either option, as regulations differ between carriers.

data.title: Canada GDP Edges Up After Sharp Contraction in October — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's economy is on track to shrink slightly in the final quarter of 2025, with the steepest contraction in almost three years notched in October and early signs pointing to only a modest recovery last month.

Gross domestic product decreased 0.3% in October from the month before, Statistics Canada said Tuesday. That reverses the 0.2% advance in September and marks the biggest step down for the economy since the end of 2022.

The national data agency's advance estimate for November indicates growth of 0.1%.

The final major economic indicator of the year sounded a sour note after recent jobs data showed hiring has picked up strongly in recent months, helping cool a still-elevated unemployment rate, and inflation figures show price pressures are largely contained.

The data on GDP, a broad measure of the goods and services produced across the economy, suggests the economy carried little to no momentum after the stronger-than-expected rebound in activity in the third quarter. If December were to show no growth, the current pace would have the economy headed for a contraction of about 0.5% on an annualized basis for the fourth quarter.

"The Canadian economy has some work cut out to avoid another negative print for the final quarter of the year. That will close out a very choppy year for Canadian growth in still-choppy fashion," Robert Kavcic, senior economist at Bank of Montreal Capital Markets, said.

The Bank of Canada, which has signaled it is done lowering interest rates after a string of aggressive moves, has forecast the economy will grow a modest 0.5% on an annual basis in the final quarter of 2025 and then expand an average 1.4% over 2026 and 2027 as weakness in the labor market weighs on household spending. The economy's potential has been badly dented by the trade conflict with the U.S. and the heightened uncertainty that has trailed President Trump's changeable tariffs.

Statistics Canada's flash estimate for November indicated recoveries in educational services, construction and transportation. That looks to have been partially offset by weakness in mining and oil-and-gas extraction and a further decline in manufacturing, which has faced a particularly rough year thanks to tariffs.

The data agency on Tuesday said an advance tally of wholesale receipts showed sales ticked up 0.1% on the month before in November, while its prior estimate for retail trade pointed to a 1.2% rise in sales, which would be the strongest advance in five months.

October's industry accounts showed a broad decline in goods-producing industries, led by a drop in manufacturing that largely offset the sector's expansion the month before.

There also was weakness during the month for the oil-and-gas industry following four straight months of expansion, and construction activity declined for the first time in six months.

Labor disputes also played a part in October's pullback. A province-wide strike in Alberta for much of October led to a contraction for the public sector, while a nationwide strike by Canada Post carriers disrupted deliveries as a walkout that began in late September shifted to rotating strikes Oct. 11.

On the positive side for the economy, the Toronto Blue Jays' World Series playoff run was likely behind strength in the arts, entertainment and recreation sector and in accommodation and food-services activity. And the finance-and-insurance industry saw a fifth consecutive monthly increase, with activity hitting a fresh record high.

The data shows economic activity remains volatile, and that the recovery from the hit taken from U.S. tariffs, which kicked in earlier in the year, remains uncertain.

Michael Davenport, senior Canada economist at Oxford Economics, said growth is likely to be restricted to the low-single-digits range on a quarterly basis in the near term, leaving the economy vulnerable to further trade disruptions and other shocks. "Although the economy avoided a technical recession in 2025, we expect weak underlying momentum to carry through the first half 2026 amid U.S. tariffs, elevated trade policy uncertainty and a shrinking population," he said.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Canada Budget Deficit Widens in October From Year Ago
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada's budget deficit widened in October, as expenditures slowed but still outstripped tepid revenue growth.

The budget deficit for October increased over 50% from year-earlier levels to 2.28 billion Canadian dollars, or the equivalent of $1.66 billion, according to the Department of Finance's monthly fiscal monitor publication. After the first seven months of the 2025-26 fiscal year, Canada's federal government ran a deficit of C$18.37 billion, versus C$14.50 billion in the comparable year-ago period.

Total expenditures on government programs and administration climbed 3.1% in October from a year ago, representing a deceleration from 4.6% growth in the prior month. The finance ministry said public-debt charges rose 2.1% in October, thereby bringing total expenses in October to C$43.82 billion, or up 3.2% from a year ago.

On the revenue side, tax receipts climbed 4.1% in October from a year ago, on solid gains in corporate and sales-tax proceeds. But the end of the consumer carbon tax, unveiled in March, led to a nearly C$1 billion decline from a year ago. As a result, total revenue in October rose 1.4% to C$41.54 billion.

The budget deficit for the current fiscal year is forecast to hit C$78.30 billion, or 2.5% of the country's gross domestic product. Excluding the pandemic period, the projected 2025-26 deficit would represent one of the biggest shortfalls, as a share of the economy, since the 2008-09 global financial crisis.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Canada April-October budget deficit grows by almost 27%
data.description: OTTAWA, Dec 23 (Reuters) - Canada recorded a C$18.37 billion ($13.40 billion) budget deficit for the first seven months of the 2025/26 fiscal year as government expenditures grew faster than revenues, the finance ministry said on Tuesday. That represents a 26.6% increase from the C$14.50 billion deficit amassed in the period from April to October 2024. Program expenses rose by 4.0%, reflecting an increase in major transfers to persons and provinces, as well as direct program expenses, the finance ministry said. Revenues posted a more modest 2.3% gain, reflecting increases in customs import duties due to countermeasures imposed in response to U.S. tariffs, and corporate and personal income tax revenue. Public debt charges dipped by 1.6%, led by lower short-term interest rates on treasury bills and lower net interest on currency swap transactions. On a monthly basis, Canada posted a deficit of C$2.28 billion in October, compared to the C$1.49 billion shortfall in October 2024. ($1 = 1.3712 Canadian dollars) (Reporting by David Ljunggren, editing by Fergal Smith) ((Reuters Ottawa bureau david.ljunggren@tr.com))

data.title: Pomerantz LLP Issues Important Reminder to Shareholders in Freeport-McMoran Inc of Class Action Filing – FCX
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / December 23, 2025 / Pomerantz LLP announces that a class action lawsuit has been filed against Freeport-McMoran Inc. ("Freeport" or the "Company") . Such investors are advised to contact Danielle Peyton at newaction@pomlaw.com or 646-581-9980, (or 888.4-POMLAW), toll-free, Ext. 7980. Those who inquire by e-mail are encouraged to include their mailing address, telephone number, and the number of shares purchased.

The class action concerns whether Freeport and certain of its officers and/or directors have engaged in securities fraud or other unlawful business practices.

You have until January 12, 2026 to ask the Court to appoint you as Lead Plaintiff for the class if you purchased or otherwise acquired Freeport securities during the Class Period. A copy of the Complaint can be obtained at www.pomerantzlaw.com.

[Click here for information about joining the class action]

On September 9, 2025, Freeport issued a press release announcing the suspension of mining activities at its Grasberg Block Cave operation in Indonesia, after "a large flow of wet material from a production drawpoint . . . blocked access to certain areas within the mine," trapping seven workers. The press release further stated that mining operations in the area "have been temporarily suspended to prioritize the safe evacuation of the . . . workers."

On this news, Freeport's stock price fell $2.80 per share, or 5.99%, to close at $43.87 per share on September 9, 2025.

Then, on September 24, 2025, Freeport provided an update on the incident, reporting that two of the seven workers were "fatally injured" and that the remaining five workers "remain missing." The press release further stated that mining operations in the area remained suspended.

On this news, Freeport's stock price fell $7.69 per share, or 16.95%, to close at $37.67 per share on September 24, 2025.

Pomerantz LLP, with offices in New York, Chicago, Los Angeles, London, Paris, and Tel Aviv, is acknowledged as one of the premier firms in the areas of corporate, securities, and antitrust class litigation. Founded by the late Abraham L. Pomerantz, known as the dean of the class action bar, Pomerantz pioneered the field of securities class actions. Today, more than 85 years later, Pomerantz continues in the tradition he established, fighting for the rights of the victims of securities fraud, breaches of fiduciary duty, and corporate misconduct. The Firm has recovered numerous multimillion-dollar damages awards on behalf of class members. See www.pomlaw.com.

Attorney advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

SOURCE: Pomerantz LLP

data.title: $1.12 Billion in Five Weeks: XRP Community Reacts to ETF Milestone
data.description:
The group of five XRP spot ETFs that have launched since Nov. 13, notably those from Canary, 21Shares, Grayscale, Bitwise and Franklin Templeton have surpassed $1.12 billion in cumulative total net inflow as of Dec. 22, according to Sosovalue.

XRP enthusiast JacktheRippler spotlights this milestone in a recent tweet, implying it to mean that institutions are accumulating XRP.

JackTheRippler ©️
@RippleXrpie

🚨BREAKING: #XRP SPOT ETFs have purchased $1.12 billion worth of XRP in just five weeks!

💥INSTITUTIONS ACCUMULATING XRP💥 pic.twitter.com/XtrX3sk9eN

Dec 23, 2025

According to Sosovalue, total net assets across spot XRP ETFs have surpassed $1.25 billion, a remarkable indication of institutional interest. 

XRP spot ETFs have attracted net inflows every trading day since their debut, sustaining an unbroken 33 day inflow streak. This separates them from Bitcoin and Ethereum ETFs that saw several sessions of outflows in recent weeks.

While Bitcoin ETFs have seen outflows in several sessions since the past month, XRP funds, by comparison, have attracted smaller but more consistent inflows.

XRP attracts inflows as Ethereum, Bitcoin bleed

According to the most recent CoinShares data, digital asset investment products recorded outflows for the first time in four weeks, totaling $952 million. The U.S. alone saw $990 million in outflows. This reflected a negative market reaction to delays in passing the U.S. Clarity Act, alongside concerns over continued selling by whale investors.

Ethereum saw the largest outflows, totaling $555 million, given it has the most to gain or lose from the Clarity Act, while Bitcoin saw $460 million. The opposite is seen for XRP, which rather continued to attract inflows coming in at $62.9 million, indicating selective investor support.

XRP sentiment slips, but it's all bullish

According to Santiment, XRP is seeing far more negative social media commentary than average. It notes that, historically, this setup leads to price rises, adding that when retail has doubts about a coin's ability to rise, the rise becomes significantly more likely.

XRP is trading down 1.41% in the last 24 hours to $1.88, extending its drop into the third day from a high of $1.95 on Dec. 20.

Amid the price drop, Santiment observes that XRP sentiment has slipped back to negative. However, this presents a silver lining: A sentiment drop into the bearish zone increases the likelihood of a strong price increase.

data.title: U.S. Mid-Atlantic Factory Activity Stays Slow Despite Brightening Outlook — Richmond Fed
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

Manufacturing activity in the mid-Atlantic region contracted again, but at a less sharp clip this month, as conditions broadly improved and expectations for future business picked up pace.

The Fifth District Survey of Manufacturing Activity's composite manufacturing index rose eight points to minus 7 in December, the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond said Tuesday. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected a weaker minus 10.

The reading below zero means more of the 149-165 firms responding to the survey reported decreasing activity than those reporting an increase. The last increase was in February.

All three of the component indexes--shipments, new orders and employment--increased from November but remained negative, the Richmond Fed said.

The index for local current business conditions climbed to minus 9 from minus 20 in November, though still registered a contraction in December. However, for local business sixth months from now, expectations gathered pace to a reading of 16 in December, from 1 last month, the survey said.

Meanwhile, average growth rates of prices decreased slightly in December, while firms expected increases in prices paid to moderate and growth in prices received to fall slightly over the next 12 months, according to the Richmond Fed.

Firms surveyed are located within the Fifth Federal Reserve District, which includes the District of Columbia, Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, Virginia, and most of West Virginia.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com
